modules.module: M1: What AI Actually Is — and Isn't

Claude.notes: Solid business-book style content with well-structured labs and coherent frameworks (Three-Layer, Bottleneck Audit, RCTC prompting), but the 'story' beats are case-study exhibits rather than problem-creating narrative, and there's no recurring protagonist across the four lessons.
Claude.topIssue: No consistent character carries the arc — each lesson opens with a different real-world vignette (BuzzFeed, Sweetgreen, GitHub, Samsung) that illustrates the concept rather than lets the learner discover it, thinning D1 narrative integrity.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of LLM, hallucination, and prompt-injection are accurate and pitched appropriately; the misconception-prevention posture is strong. However, several precisely-cited statistics (HubSpot 4x retention, Zapier 60% onboarding reduction, McKinsey 22/35/40% figures, GitHub Copilot 55% first-time-working) read as paraphrased or composed rather than directly traceable to public research.
Gemini.topIssue: Multiple 'research-cited' percentages (Zapier 60%, HubSpot 4x, McKinsey time-savings triad, GitHub 55%) are stated with authority but are not verifiable against the published research those organizations released — the quiz then tests recall of those numbers, compounding the accuracy risk.

Perplexity.notes: Frameworks and labs remain executable with today's tools, and the ChatGPT/Jasper/Harvey/Domo tool set is still recognizable. But the module — dated April 2026 — reads as if authored in 2024: it never mentions o1-class reasoning models, GPT-4o/Claude 3.7-era capabilities, MCP, agentic workflows, the EU AI Act, or California ADMT/AB 1008. The 'current' foundation-model list (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama) omits the model generations that actually dominate 2026 buying decisions.
Perplexity.topIssue: Every cited case (BuzzFeed 2023, Air Canada, Sweetgreen 2023, GitHub 2023, Samsung 2023, HubSpot 2023) is 2–3 years old with no 2025–2026 anchors — the SMB AI landscape has shifted materially since (Copilot Studio, ChatGPT Team/Enterprise, agents, EU AI Act enforcement) and the module ignores it.

Claude.notes: Solid concept architecture and lab executability, but the module reads as concept-with-anecdotes rather than story-driven. Case studies illustrate rather than create the problem, and no protagonist arc carries the learner across lessons.
Claude.topIssue: Story density is roughly 20% (Advanced ratio) while cognitive framing is Basic; the learner is told the insight through case-study vignettes rather than arriving at it via a character's journey.

Gemini.notes: Frameworks (Three-Zone, Impact-Effort, four criteria) are pedagogically sound but the module is loaded with suspiciously precise statistics attributed to McKinsey, Xero, Salesforce, Square, and ADA that do not clearly map to verifiable publications. Quiz questions test recall of these unverified stats.
Gemini.topIssue: Multiple citations (McKinsey 2022 '40% higher ROI', Xero '104 hours', Salesforce 2023 '52%', Square '2.3x/68%', ADA 2021 dental practice stats) appear fabricated or heavily composited; the module test rewards memorization of them.

Perplexity.notes: Tools named (NexHealth, Weave, HubSpot, QuickBooks AI, Shopify analytics) are current and appropriate for 2025-26 small business context, and the lab work is genuinely doable with today's AI chat tools. However, the specific case-study statistics feel manufactured rather than sourced.
Perplexity.topIssue: Case-study precision does not match the AI landscape of the last 12 months — no verifiable Square 2023 Future of Commerce finding matches the '68% more likely / 2.3x satisfaction' claim, and Maine Lobster Now's '40% more order volume' quote is unattributable.

modules.module: M3: Writing AI-Assisted Job Descriptions and Screening Criteria

Claude.notes: Case-study vignettes (Sweetgreen, Hilton, Amazon, McDonald's, NYC Law 144) do create the problem the concept solves, but each lesson swaps in a new real-world case rather than following a persistent protagonist, so the learner is told the moral instead of arriving at it.
Claude.topIssue: Insights are handed to the learner via italicized 'The lesson was...' summaries at the end of each vignette; there is no sustained character whose choices force the reader to derive the principle themselves.

Gemini.notes: Legal and technical concepts (Title VII disparate impact, EEOC 2023 guidance, STAR, ADEA 40-and-over) are handled accurately, but two of the anchoring case studies contain material factual weaknesses that a reader could take away as fact.
Gemini.topIssue: Sweetgreen's 2023 AI job-drafting rollout across 220+ locations is not publicly documented, and Dynamic Yield's 2019 acquisition was primarily for drive-through/menu personalization — the workforce-scheduling 2–4% labor-cost figure is presented without a verifiable source.

Perplexity.notes: Labs are directly executable via the embedded assistant and the mentioned SMB tools (7shifts, Homebase, When I Work, HireVue, Notion, 15Five) are still current, but the regulatory landscape has moved past what's cited — the module reads as a 2023 snapshot in a 2026 course.
Perplexity.topIssue: Missing the Colorado AI Act (2024, hiring high-risk), the EU AI Act's high-risk classification for hiring (Aug 2024), and California's ADS regulations; also fails to name any specific consumer AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot) that the learner should actually open when they leave the lab sandbox.

modules.module: M4: Personalization at Scale: How AI Learns What Your Customer Wants

Claude.notes: Content-rich Basic-level lesson set with strong opener cases, but the module is concept-heavy and story-light (each lesson opens with a brief real-case vignette and then reverts to expository prose), missing the 50-65% narrative density target for Basic.
Claude.topIssue: Narrative density falls well below the Basic target — story-scenes are used only as openers, and there are no recurring characters or continuing narrative through the concept sections.

Gemini.notes: Collaborative/content/hybrid distinctions are cleanly explained and technically correct; RFM and containment-vs-repeat-contact framing are accurate. Several cited statistics are plausible but weakly sourced (T-Mobile 1.69%→0.98% figures conflate churn segments; the Luca & Zervas 2018 review-response citation appears to reference the wrong paper — the manager-response finding is Proserpio & Zervas).
Gemini.topIssue: Multiple documented figures are presented with false specificity — Luca & Zervas 2018 citation is misattributed and the T-Mobile Q1 2015 churn baseline of 1.69% does not match published postpaid churn data.

Perplexity.notes: Tool stack (Klaviyo, Shopify, LoyaltyLion, Intercom Fin, Tidio, Yotpo, Birdeye, etc.) is current and accessible to small businesses today, and labs are genuinely doable with an off-the-shelf GPT-4o assistant. Most flagship cases (Klarna, Sephora, McDonald's/Dynamic Yield) are 2+ years old — no evidence of 2025-2026 refresh with newer agentic-AI or GPT-5-era SMB deployments.
Perplexity.topIssue: Currency window drift — as of 2026-07 the newest documented case is Klarna Feb 2024; module has not been refreshed to reflect the last 12 months of SMB personalization/agentic-support tooling.

Claude.notes: Concept content and labs are strong, but narrative is limited to opening 'context' vignettes with no recurring characters and no story that drives the concept payoff. Story-concept flow feels bolted-on rather than integrated.
Claude.topIssue: No recurring character or narrative thread carries across lessons; story-scenes function as journalistic case openers rather than a driving problem the learner solves.

Gemini.notes: Correctly names gradient-boosted trees and LSTMs as workhorses and defines MAPE, EOQ, ROP accurately, but omits Prophet/ARIMA which dominate practitioner tooling; Pop-Tarts anecdote is treated more literally than the underlying case supports.
Gemini.topIssue: Model coverage skips Prophet and ARIMA — the two most common forecasting methods small operators actually encounter in Excel/Python tutorials — while presenting LSTMs as typical for small-business tools where they are overkill.

Perplexity.notes: Tool references reflect current small-business reality (Cin7/DEAR, Extensiv/Skubana, Returnly/Affirm, Sphera acquisition of Riskmethods all correctly noted), prices are plausible, and lab system prompts are executable with concrete tool + budget guidance. Narrative remains the weak spot.
Perplexity.topIssue: Quiz #12's '96% would buy again from an easy-returns retailer' Narvar figure is presented as canonical but this Narvar stat has bounced between reports — worth verifying the exact 2022 source before shipping.

Claude.notes: Strong case-study openings drive each lesson, but no recurring protagonist across the four lessons weakens narrative cohesion. Concept density skews higher than the Basic target of 50-65%.
Claude.topIssue: No sustained character arc — each lesson swaps in a new company case study, breaking narrative continuity across the module.

Gemini.notes: Technical definitions (CCC, NPV, elasticity) are accurate, and the NPV worked example in the L4 system prompt correctly computes to ~$96.5K. Regression/scenario language is used precisely, though Monte Carlo is not introduced.
Gemini.topIssue: Several precise statistics (Fathom 34% variance reduction, Inventory Planner 23%, Ramp $63K) are cited as research findings but read like vendor marketing claims — sourcing rigor should be tightened.

Perplexity.notes: Tool inventory is current and well-mapped to small-business reality: Copilot for Finance, QuickBooks AI, Fathom, Jirav, Float, Ramp, Brex, Cin7, Tesorio, Versapay. Labs give managers concrete, executable scenarios with dollar figures.
Perplexity.topIssue: Missing coverage of Excel Copilot for finance workflows and 2024-2025 Intuit Assist evolutions — the toolset skews 2022-2023 vintage in a fast-moving space.

modules.module: M7: Why People Resist AI — and What Actually Works

Claude.notes: Stories genuinely dramatize resistance (Amazon slowdowns, Google Aristotle, Klarna, Watson) and each opens its lesson with real stakes. Narrative density is thinner than a Basic module ideally wants — story cold-opens fade quickly into expository research prose.
Claude.topIssue: Narrative density lands closer to Advanced (~25-30%) than the Basic target (50-65%); stories are punchy openers but rarely return to carry the concept through.

Gemini.notes: Core definitions are accurate — ADKAR sequence, Edmondson's precise psych-safety definition, Rogers' categories, and Bansal et al.'s appropriate-reliance concept are all rendered correctly. Several oddly specific stats (HubSpot 2.4×, Shopify 68%, Forrester 61%, Prosci 40%, Gartner 34%) are presented with confident attribution but are hard to verify against primary sources.
Gemini.topIssue: Named-vendor statistics (HubSpot 2.4×, Shopify 68% error-hunt lift, Forrester 61%, Prosci 40% resistance reduction) are attributed with report-level specificity but lack citations traceable to a public benchmark — risk of quiet fabrication.

Perplexity.notes: Change-management fundamentals (ADKAR, Rogers, Edmondson) are timeless and correctly used, but the AI-adoption cases are heavily 2023-early-2024 — a lifetime ago in this space. Klarna's Feb 2024 announcement is now the textbook example of an overstated AI rollback: they publicly walked it back in mid-2025, which this module does not acknowledge.
Perplexity.topIssue: Klarna 2024 is presented as a stable success story; in 2026 the honest reading is that Klarna reversed course and re-hired humans — omitting the 2025 walk-back leaves learners with a stale, misleading anchor case.

modules.module: M8: Auditing Your Business for AI Opportunity

Claude.notes: Strong capstone architecture — four sequenced artifacts (audit, matrix, workflow doc, dashboard) build one coherent playbook. Case-study vignettes replace recurring characters, weakening arc cohesion for a supposed capstone.
Claude.topIssue: No persistent narrative character or storyline across the four lessons — each lesson jumps to a new company (Brava, Shopify, River Valley, Main Street Hub), so the module reads as sequential case studies rather than a unified capstone arc.

Gemini.notes: Frameworks (opportunity score, Three-Gate Test, HITL levels, four KPIs) are internally consistent and pedagogically sound. Misconception prevention (calibrate review to error cost not volume; cost per task not monthly headline) is excellent.
Gemini.topIssue: Several load-bearing statistics — Goldman Sachs 2.3× gain, Main Street Hub 78%/54%, Shopify 37%/51%/18%, MIT Sloan 40% incident reduction, SBA 23% admin time — are cited with suspicious precision and no linked source; risk of fabricated-sounding attribution that a working manager could not verify.

Perplexity.notes: Labs are fully executable with today's stack — ChatGPT, Claude, HubSpot, Mailchimp AI, Jasper, Zapier, Make, Notion, Sheets are all live and appropriate for small-business scale. Peer-network sourcing (SCORE, 10KSB, trade associations) is current and correct.
Perplexity.topIssue: Quiz and module-test items skew heavily toward recall of specific case-study numbers (14 hrs / 12 categories, 51%, 78%/54%, 40%, 2.3×) rather than judgment application — a manager who understands the frameworks could still fail the module test on trivia, and the trivia won't help them in practice.
